verses.text: Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh: the LORD God of Israel says, let My people go to hold a feast to Me in the wilderness. Pharaoh: who is the LORD? I do not know Him, and I will not let Israel go. Guidance: When God's name is unknown to power, the request meets arrogance, not negotiation. Don't be shocked when the world refuses to honor what you have learned to revere.

Moses and Aaron go to Pharaoh: the LORD God of Israel says, let My people go to hold a feast to Me in the wilderness. Pharaoh: who is the LORD? I do not know Him, and I will not let Israel go. Guidance: When God's name is unknown to power, the request meets arrogance, not negotiation. Don't be shocked when the world refuses to honor what you have learned to revere.

The God of the Hebrews has met with us, they continue. Let us go three days into the wilderness to sacrifice — lest He strike us with plague or sword. Guidance: God's people often soften their first request before power. The boldness Moses learns will come from God's continued presence, not from his own grit.

Pharaoh: Moses, Aaron — why are you turning the people from their work? Get back to your labors. The people of the land are now many; you are giving them rest from their burdens. Guidance: Resistance to God's call often comes wrapped in economic complaints. Pharaoh sees Hebrew freedom as Egypt's loss.

Pharaoh: Moses, Aaron — why are you turning the people from their work? Get back to your labors. The people of the land are now many; you are giving them rest from their burdens. Guidance: Resistance to God's call often comes wrapped in economic complaints. Pharaoh sees Hebrew freedom as Egypt's loss.

That same day Pharaoh orders the taskmasters: stop giving the people straw. Let them gather it themselves — but the daily quota stays the same. They are idle; that is why they cry let us go sacrifice. Pile on more work; let them not heed empty words. Guidance: Oppression often escalates the moment hope is named. The first effect of speaking up for the right thing is sometimes a heavier load.

That same day Pharaoh orders the taskmasters: stop giving the people straw. Let them gather it themselves — but the daily quota stays the same. They are idle; that is why they cry let us go sacrifice. Pile on more work; let them not heed empty words. Guidance: Oppression often escalates the moment hope is named. The first effect of speaking up for the right thing is sometimes a heavier load.

The taskmasters tell the people: Pharaoh will not give you straw. Go gather it yourselves — yet the daily output shall not be diminished. The people scatter across Egypt looking for stubble. The taskmasters hurry them: meet the daily quota as if you still had straw. Guidance: Cruelty multiplies through middle managers who carry out orders without questioning them. The system depends on people doing what they are told.

The taskmasters hurry them: meet the daily quota as if you still had straw. Guidance: Cruelty multiplies through middle managers who carry out orders without questioning them. The system depends on people doing what they are told.

The Hebrew officers — placed in charge by Pharaoh's taskmasters — are beaten for failing to deliver the quota. Why have you not made your bricks yesterday and today as before? Guidance: When the structure traps you, you suffer twice: once from those above, once from those who are also trapped. Compassion for middle layers takes practice.

The officers cry out to Pharaoh: why are you treating your servants this way? You give us no straw, yet they tell us, make bricks. We are beaten. The fault is in your own people. Guidance: Sometimes appealing up the ladder is the only step left. Even when the appeal fails, naming the injustice out loud is part of resistance.

The officers cry out to Pharaoh: why are you treating your servants this way? You give us no straw, yet they tell us, make bricks. We are beaten. The fault is in your own people. Guidance: Sometimes appealing up the ladder is the only step left. Even when the appeal fails, naming the injustice out loud is part of resistance.

Pharaoh: you are idle, that is why you say, let us go sacrifice. Go now and work. No straw, but the same brick quota. The officers saw they were in trouble — the order would not change. Guidance: Power often calls grief and protest by another name — laziness, ingratitude, exaggeration. Don't be quick to accept the label power puts on your pain.

Pharaoh: you are idle, that is why you say, let us go sacrifice. Go now and work. No straw, but the same brick quota. The officers saw they were in trouble — the order would not change. Guidance: Power often calls grief and protest by another name — laziness, ingratitude, exaggeration. Don't be quick to accept the label power puts on your pain.

Pharaoh: you are idle, that is why you say, let us go sacrifice. Go now and work. No straw, but the same brick quota. The officers saw they were in trouble — the order would not change. Guidance: Power often calls grief and protest by another name — laziness, ingratitude, exaggeration. Don't be quick to accept the label power puts on your pain.

The officers find Moses and Aaron waiting outside, and they let them have it: the LORD look upon you and judge — because you have made our smell abhorrent to Pharaoh and his servants, and put a sword in their hand to kill us. Guidance: When obedience makes things worse before it makes them better, the people you came to help may turn on you first. Stay anyway.

The officers find Moses and Aaron waiting outside, and they let them have it: the LORD look upon you and judge — because you have made our smell abhorrent to Pharaoh and his servants, and put a sword in their hand to kill us. Guidance: When obedience makes things worse before it makes them better, the people you came to help may turn on you first. Stay anyway.

Moses returns to the LORD: Lord, why have You done evil to this people? Why did You send me? Since I came to speak in Your name, Pharaoh has done evil to them, and You have not delivered them at all. Guidance: Honest prayer can include accusation. Moses speaks his disappointment directly. God can handle the questions of the called as well as their obedience.
